grounding_note: 'I need help with my mother's estate' is the archetypal ambiguous estate intake: whether the person is alive (planning: wills/trusts/POA), deceased with court administration needed (Probate), assets held in trust (Living Trusts/administration), or heirs fighting (W&T Litigation) are mutually exclusive procedural postures. Row 412 split raters across Foreclosure/Probate/W&T Litigation for exactly this reason. Users always know these facts.

scenarios.opening_query: I need help with my mother's estate. There's a house, some savings, and a lot of paperwork I don't understand.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > General (Wills/Trusts/Estates) || Wills & Trusts > Probate
scenarios.hidden_fact: Mom passed away in April; the bank says we need court authority before anyone can touch the accounts.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: She passed in April — the bank says we need court authority before touching anything.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the parent is living (planning) or deceased (estate administration)
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Mom is alive and doing okay — she wants everything organized now so we're not lost later.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She's alive and well — she just wants everything organized ahead of time.
scenarios.vetting_note: The single most common estate ambiguity in real intake; alive/deceased is decisive and always known.

scenarios.opening_query: We need a lawyer to handle my father's affairs. It's more than the family can manage alone.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Power of Attorney || Wills & Trusts > General (Wills/Trusts/Estates)
scenarios.hidden_fact: Dad is alive but declining — he's still lucid and wants to appoint my sister to handle finances before it's too late.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's alive — declining, but lucid — and wants my sister formally appointed to handle his finances while he still can.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the father is deceased or living and able to sign planning documents
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Dad died in January without a will, and the county says the estate has to go through court.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He died in January with no will — the county says it has to go through court.
scenarios.vetting_note: 'Handle his affairs' spans death administration and incapacity planning; posture fact routes it.

scenarios.opening_query: My grandmother's house needs to be dealt with and the family doesn't agree on anything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: My uncle already filed a court challenge claiming the will was signed under pressure — there's a contested hearing set.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My uncle filed a challenge saying the will was signed under pressure — there's a contested hearing scheduled.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the disagreement has become a formal will contest
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Nobody's filed anything — we just need the estate opened and the house transferred the way the will says.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No one's filed anything — we just need the estate opened and the house handled per the will.
scenarios.vetting_note: Family friction vs filed contest: the pending-challenge fact converts administration into litigation (row 412's split).

scenarios.opening_query: I was named to handle my brother's estate and I have no idea what I'm supposed to do first.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Living Trusts
scenarios.hidden_fact: Everything he owned is titled in his revocable living trust, and I'm the successor trustee — the lawyer who drafted it retired.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Everything's in his revocable living trust and I'm successor trustee — the drafting lawyer retired.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether assets pass through a trust or require court probate
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There's just a will — no trust — and the court sent me forms about being appointed personal representative.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Just a will, no trust — the court sent me personal-representative forms.
scenarios.vetting_note: Trust administration vs probate is invisible until the titling fact is disclosed; both are 'handling the estate' to users.

scenarios.opening_query: My parents want to get their affairs in order and asked me to find them a lawyer.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > General (Wills/Trusts/Estates) || Wills & Trusts > Medicare/Medicaid Planning
scenarios.hidden_fact: The real driver is Dad's Parkinson's — we need their savings structured so he can qualify for Medicaid when he needs nursing care.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The real reason is Dad's Parkinson's — they need savings structured so Medicaid will cover nursing care later.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the planning goal is qualifying for long-term-care benefits
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: They're healthy — they just want simple wills and beneficiary designations done properly.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They're healthy — just want proper wills and beneficiaries set up.
scenarios.vetting_note: The taxonomy explicitly distinguishes Medicaid asset planning from general estate planning; the health/goal fact selects.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm a beneficiary of my aunt's estate and I haven't received anything or heard anything in over a year.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: The executor is her boyfriend, and county records show he transferred her car and cabin to himself before filing anything.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The executor is her boyfriend — records show he moved her car and cabin into his own name before filing anything.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the fiduciary has engaged in self-dealing requiring suit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The executor is my cousin who is just overwhelmed and slow — nothing improper, we just need the process moved along.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The executor's my cousin — just overwhelmed, nothing shady. We need it moved along.
scenarios.vetting_note: Delay vs self-dealing: the records fact converts patience into a fiduciary suit.

scenarios.opening_query: My grandfather left me and my niece the family home but there are complications with taking it over.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Real Property > Foreclosure
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'complication' is a reverse mortgage — the lender has already scheduled a foreclosure auction unless we act fast.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a reverse mortgage — the lender already scheduled a foreclosure auction unless we move fast.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a lender is actively foreclosing on the inherited property
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The house is paid off — the complication is just that the deed transfer needs the estate opened first.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The house is paid off — we just need the estate opened to transfer the deed.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 412 verbatim: inherited home with auction pending split raters between Probate and Foreclosure; urgency fact decides.

scenarios.opening_query: We're deciding whether to transfer ownership of a property or put it in a trust, and we need advice.
scenarios.final: Real Property > Government Loans (VA, FHA, etc.)
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > General (Wills/Trusts/Estates) || Real Property > General (Residential) || Real Property > Government Loans (VA, FHA, etc.)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The property carries a VA construction loan tied to my husband's disabled-veteran status, and the transfer can't jeopardize that loan or his property-tax exemption.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: There's a VA construction loan tied to my husband's disabled-veteran status — the transfer can't endanger that loan or his tax exemption.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a government-backed loan constrains the property transfer
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The property is owned free and clear — it's purely an estate-planning choice.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's owned free and clear — purely an estate-planning question.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 384 verbatim scenario: the VA-loan fact is what pulled human raters toward the Government Loans label.

scenarios.opening_query: My sister has been living in our late mother's house and refuses to discuss selling or splitting anything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Litigation || Real Property > Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: The estate actually closed two years ago and we own it 50/50 on the deed — I need to force a sale through a partition case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The estate closed two years ago — we're both on the deed 50/50, so I need to force a sale.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the estate is still open or the fight is between co-owners on the deed
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The estate was never opened at all — the house is still in Mom's name and nothing can happen until that's fixed.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The estate was never opened — the house is still in Mom's name.
scenarios.vetting_note: Whether title has passed determines probate vs partition litigation; a pure procedural-posture fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I want to make sure my disabled son is taken care of after I'm gone. Where do I start?
scenarios.final: Wills & Trusts > Special Needs/Inc. Cap Trusts
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > General (Wills/Trusts/Estates) || Wills & Trusts > Special Needs/Inc. Cap Trusts
scenarios.hidden_fact: He receives SSI and Medicaid, and I've been warned a direct inheritance would disqualify him from both.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's on SSI and Medicaid — I've been told leaving him money directly would disqualify him from both.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the beneficiary receives needs-based benefits an inheritance would jeopardize
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He doesn't receive any government benefits — his disability is managed and he works; I just want a standard will naming him.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He's not on any benefits — he works. I just want a standard will naming him.
scenarios.vetting_note: Benefits status is the fact that mandates a special-needs trust; the taxonomy has a dedicated label for exactly this.

scenarios.opening_query: My father remarried late in life and now his new wife controls everything he owned. We're worried.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Litigation || Wills & Trusts > Elder Abuse
scenarios.hidden_fact: Dad has advancing dementia, and she had him sign a new will and deed at home two months after his diagnosis, with her own notary.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Dad has dementia — she had him sign a new will and deed at home right after the diagnosis, using her own notary.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether documents were changed while the elder lacked capacity under pressure
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Dad passed last month fully sharp — the will is old and valid; our dispute is with how she's administering it as executor.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He passed last month, sharp to the end — the will's old and valid. Our issue is how she's running the estate.
scenarios.vetting_note: Capacity-plus-pressure is the taxonomy's elder-abuse core; without it the same family fight is estate litigation.

scenarios.opening_query: The IRS says money is owed on what my uncle left behind and the family is arguing about who pays.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Taxation > Estate & Gift
scenarios.hidden_fact: The estate itself was settled fine — the fight is the IRS's estate-tax calculation, which double-counts a farm valuation, and we need that contested.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The estate settled fine — the problem is the IRS's estate-tax math double-counting the farm, and we need to contest it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the dispute is estate administration or the tax liability itself
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The tax notice exists because nobody ever opened the estate or filed anything — we need the administration done properly first.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The notice came because no one ever opened the estate — we need the administration done right first.
scenarios.vetting_note: Taxonomy separates who-inherits (W&T) from tax-owed (Taxation > Estate & Gift); the settled-vs-unopened fact routes.
